q:
An LCR circuit is equivalent to a damped pendulum. In an LCR circuit the capacitor is charged to Q0 and then connected to the L and R as shown below:

If a student plots graphs of the square of maximum charge QMax2 on the capacitor with time (t) for two different values L1 and L2(L1>L2) of L then which of the following represents this graph correctly? (plots are schematic and not drawn to scale)
question:
An LCR circuit is equivalent to a damped pendulum. In an LCR circuit the capacitor is charged to Q0 and then connected to the L and R as shown below:

If a student plots graphs of the square of maximum charge QMax2 on the capacitor with time (t) for two different values L1 and L2(L1>L2) of L then which of the following represents this graph correctly? (plots are schematic and not drawn to scale)
solution:
Comparing to the damped pendulum. We write, mdvdt=-kx-bv, bv is resistive force, ⇒  Amplitude, A=A0e-bt2m. Comparing results, we can write

qc=+Ldidt+iR, as charge decreasing, 


qc=Ld2qdt2-dqdtR

⇒A=q; R=b, m=L
⇒q=q0e-Rt2L⇒q2=q02e-RtL⇒q2=q02e-RtL.

Exponentially decreasing function, L1 graph has higher values than L2.

subject: Physics
q: A capacitor of reactance $4 \sqrt{3} \Omega$ and a resistor of resistance $4 \Omega$ are connected in series with an ac source of peak value $8 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V}$. The power dissipation in the circuit is ________ W.
question: A capacitor of reactance $4 \sqrt{3} \Omega$ and a resistor of resistance $4 \Omega$ are connected in series with an ac source of peak value $8 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V}$. The power dissipation in the circuit is ________ W.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}^2 \mathrm{~L}} \\ & \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{4^2+(4 \sqrt{3})^2}=8 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}=(8 \mathrm{~V}) \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{\mathrm{Z}}=\frac{8}{8}=1 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$ Power dissipated $=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2 \times \mathrm{R}=1 \times 4=(4 \mathrm{~W})$

subject: Physics
q: The figure given below shows an $LCR$ series circuit with two switches $S_1$ and $S_2$. When switch $S_1$ is closed keeping $S_2$ open, the phase difference $(\phi)$ between the current and source voltage is $30°$ and phase difference is $60°$ when $S_2$ is closed keeping $S_1$ open. The value of $(3L_1 - L_2)$ is _______ H.
question: The figure given below shows an $LCR$ series circuit with two switches $S_1$ and $S_2$. When switch $S_1$ is closed keeping $S_2$ open, the phase difference $(\phi)$ between the current and source voltage is $30°$ and phase difference is $60°$ when $S_2$ is closed keeping $S_1$ open. The value of $(3L_1 - L_2)$ is _______ H.
solution:
From the source $v = V_0 \sin(300t)$, the angular frequency is $\omega = 300\text{ rad/s}$.
Capacitance $C = 100\ \mu\text{F} = 10^{-4}\text{ F}$, so $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$.

Case 1 ($S_1$ closed, $S_2$ open):
Closing $S_1$ short-circuits $L_2$, leaving an LCR series circuit with $C$, $R$ and $L_1$. With phase difference $\phi_1 = 30^\circ$:
$\tan 30^\circ = \dfrac{\omega L_1 - X_C}{R}$
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{\omega L_1 - X_C}{R} \Rightarrow R = \sqrt{3}\,(\omega L_1 - X_C)$ ... (1)

Case 2 ($S_2$ closed, $S_1$ open):
Closing $S_2$ short-circuits $L_1$, leaving an LCR series circuit with $C$, $R$ and $L_2$. With phase difference $\phi_2 = 60^\circ$:
$\tan 60^\circ = \dfrac{\omega L_2 - X_C}{R}$
$\sqrt{3} = \dfrac{\omega L_2 - X_C}{R} \Rightarrow \omega L_2 - X_C = R\sqrt{3}$ ... (2)

Substituting $R$ from (1) into (2):
$\omega L_2 - X_C = \sqrt{3}\,(\omega L_1 - X_C) \cdot \sqrt{3}$
$\omega L_2 - X_C = 3\omega L_1 - 3X_C$
$2X_C = 3\omega L_1 - \omega L_2 = \omega(3L_1 - L_2)$

Therefore:
$3L_1 - L_2 = \dfrac{2X_C}{\omega} = \dfrac{2}{\omega^2 C}$

Substituting $\omega = 300\text{ rad/s}$ and $C = 10^{-4}\text{ F}$:
$\omega^2 C = (300)^2 \cdot 10^{-4} = 9 \times 10^4 \cdot 10^{-4} = 9$

$3L_1 - L_2 = \dfrac{2}{9}\text{ H}$

Hence, the correct option is $(2)\ \dfrac{2}{9}$.

q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Maximum power is dissipated in a circuit containing an inductor, a capacitor and a resistor connected in series with an AC source, when resonance occurs.

Statement II: Maximum power is dissipated in a circuit containing pure resistor due to zero phase difference between current and voltage.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Maximum power is dissipated in a circuit containing an inductor, a capacitor and a resistor connected in series with an AC source, when resonance occurs.

Statement II: Maximum power is dissipated in a circuit containing pure resistor due to zero phase difference between current and voltage.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false
solution:
The formula for impedance is given by 

Z=R2+XL-XC2

At resonance, XL=XC. So, Zmin=R

Power will be maximum when impedance is minimum. Thus the maximum power occurs in a purely resistive circuit.

subject: Physics
q: A LCR series circuit driven with $E_{rms} = 90$ V at frequency $f_d = 30$ Hz has resistance $R = 80\,\Omega$, an inductance with inductive reactance $X_L = 20.0\,\Omega$ and capacitance with capacitive reactance $X_C = 80.0\,\Omega$. The power factor of the circuit is _______.
question: A LCR series circuit driven with $E_{rms} = 90$ V at frequency $f_d = 30$ Hz has resistance $R = 80\,\Omega$, an inductance with inductive reactance $X_L = 20.0\,\Omega$ and capacitance with capacitive reactance $X_C = 80.0\,\Omega$. The power factor of the circuit is _______.
solution:
The impedance of the LCR series circuit is given by $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_C - X_L)^2}$.

Substituting the given values:

$Z = \sqrt{80^2 + (80 - 20)^2}$

$Z = \sqrt{80^2 + 60^2}$

$Z = \sqrt{6400 + 3600} = \sqrt{10000} = 100\,\Omega$

The power factor of the circuit is given by $\cos \phi = \dfrac{R}{Z}$.

$\cos \phi = \dfrac{80}{100} = 0.8$

Answer: $0.8$

take π2=10
question:

take π2=10
solution:
Angular frequency, ω=2πf=2π×50=100π rad s-1

At resonance,

XC=XL⇒1ωC=ωL⇒1ωC=250π⇒C=1100π×250π F=4 μF

q: An AC source is connected to an inductance of 100 mH, a capacitance of 100 μF and a resistance of 120 Ω as shown in figure. The time in which the resistance having a thermal capacity 2 J C-1∘ will get heated by 16°C is _____ s.
question: An AC source is connected to an inductance of 100 mH, a capacitance of 100 μF and a resistance of 120 Ω as shown in figure. The time in which the resistance having a thermal capacity 2 J C-1∘ will get heated by 16°C is _____ s.
solution:
From the diagram given,

Angular frequency ω=100 rad s-1 and Vrms=V02=2022=20 V.

Now, XL=ωL=100×100×10-3=10 Ω and XC=1ωC=1100×100×10-6=100 Ω

XL-XC=10-100=90 Ω

The impedance of the circuit,

Z=XL-XC2+R2=902+1202=150 Ω

The current in the circuit,

imrs=VrmsZ=20150 A 

Power factor cosϕ=RZ

Now

VrmsirmscosϕΔt=msΔT⇒Δt=msΔTVrms×VrmsZ×RZ=msΔTZ2Vrms2×R=2×16×22500400×120=15 s

⇒Δt=15 s

q: In an LC oscillator, if values of inductance and capacitance become twice and eight times, respectively, then the resonant frequency of oscillator becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> times its initial resonant frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
question: In an LC oscillator, if values of inductance and capacitance become twice and eight times, respectively, then the resonant frequency of oscillator becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> times its initial resonant frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
solution:
Resonance frequency of an LC oscillations circuit is stated as 

ω0=1LC, here, L and C represents inductance and capacitance of circuit.

Now, when inductance and capacitance changes, i.e., L'=2L and C'=8C

Then, resonant frequency is ω=12L×8C=14LC

⇒ω=ω04

Thus, the value of x=14.

cosϕ=12

q:
An ac current is represented as
$\mathrm{i}=5 \sqrt{2}+10 \cos \left(650 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \mathrm{Amp}$
The r.m.s value of the current is
question:
An ac current is represented as
$\mathrm{i}=5 \sqrt{2}+10 \cos \left(650 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \mathrm{Amp}$
The r.m.s value of the current is
solution:
$i=5 \sqrt{2}+10 \cos \left(650 \pi t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\mathrm{i}^2=50+100 \cos ^2\left(650 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$+(2)(5 \sqrt{2})(10) \cos \left(650 \pi t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\left\langle\mathrm{i}^2\right\rangle=50+\frac{100}{2}+0$
$\left. \lt \mathrm{i}^2\right\rangle=100$
$ \lt \mathrm{i} \gt =10 \mathrm{Amp}$

The rms value of conduction current in the circuit and displacement current in the capacitor respectively are :
solution:
By Maxwell's equation for a time varying voltage (AC voltage), both conduction and displacement currents are the same. Therefore,

Idisplacement=Iconduction=VrmsXC=Vrms1ωC=230×300×200×10−12=13.8 μA

q: When you walk through a metal detector carrying a metal object in your pocket, it raises an alarm. This phenomenon works on
question: When you walk through a metal detector carrying a metal object in your pocket, it raises an alarm. This phenomenon works on
solution:
Metal detector works on the principle of transmitting an electromagnetic signal and analyses a return signal from the target. So it works on the principle of resonance in AC circuit.

When the LC circuit detects any resonating frequency from any metal which is near to it, electric field will be created which will lead to induces current in the coil and changes in the signal flow through the coil.

Now, a capacitor is added in series such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. the ratio of the new power factor with the old power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt><mo>:</mo><mi>x</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: An AC circuit has an inductor and a resistor of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> in series, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. Now, a capacitor is added in series such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. the ratio of the new power factor with the old power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt><mo>:</mo><mi>x</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
First Recall the formula of power factor, cosϕ=RZ ⇒cosϕinitial =RZ=RXL2+R2=R9R2+R2=110. 

 ⇒cosϕfinal =RZ=RXL-XC2+R2=RR2+R2=12

⇒cosϕfinal cosϕinitial =5x⇒x=1

q: A direct current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and an alternating current of peak value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flow through resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of heat produced in the two resistances in same interval of time will be :
question: A direct current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and an alternating current of peak value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flow through resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of heat produced in the two resistances in same interval of time will be :
solution:
Heat produced in resistor when direct current flows is H1=i2R1t   

H1=423t=163t

Heat produced in resistor when alternating current flows is  H2=irms2R2t                irms=i02  

H2=i022R2t

H2=422×2t=16t

Thus, the ratio is H1:H2=3:1

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of $10$ mH, capacitor of $0.1\ \mu$F and a resistor of $100\ \Omega$ are connected in series across an $a.c$ power supply $220$ V, $70$ Hz. The power factor of the given circuit is $0.5$. The difference in the inductive reactance and capacitance reactance is $\sqrt{3}\alpha\ \Omega$. The value of $\alpha$ is _____.
question: An inductor of $10$ mH, capacitor of $0.1\ \mu$F and a resistor of $100\ \Omega$ are connected in series across an $a.c$ power supply $220$ V, $70$ Hz. The power factor of the given circuit is $0.5$. The difference in the inductive reactance and capacitance reactance is $\sqrt{3}\alpha\ \Omega$. The value of $\alpha$ is _____.
solution:
The power factor of an LCR series circuit is given by $\cos \phi = \dfrac{R}{Z}$.

Given $\cos \phi = 0.5$ and $R = 100 \ \Omega$.

Substituting the values, we get:

$0.5 = \dfrac{100}{Z} \Rightarrow Z = 200 \ \Omega$

The impedance $Z$ of the circuit is also given by:

$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$

Squaring both sides and substituting the values of $Z$ and $R$:

$(200)^2 = (100)^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2$

$40000 = 10000 + (X_L - X_C)^2$

$(X_L - X_C)^2 = 30000$

$|X_L - X_C| = 100\sqrt{3} \ \Omega$

The difference in inductive and capacitive reactance is given as $\sqrt{3}\alpha \ \Omega$.

Comparing the two expressions:

$\sqrt{3}\alpha = 100\sqrt{3}$

$\alpha = 100$

Answer: $100$

q: As shown in the figure an inductor of inductance 200 mH is connected to an AC source of emf 220 Vand frequency 50 Hz. The instantaneous voltage of the source is 0 V when the peak value of current is aπ A. The value of a is _____ .

solution:
The reactance of the given inductor is,

XL=ωL=2πfL=2π×50×0.2=20π Ω.

The maximum current in the given circuit will be,

i0=2VrmsXL=2×22020π=242π A

Therefore, a=242

For maximum value of amplitude of current in circuit, the value of inductance is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mH</mi></math>.<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open=""><mrow><mo>(</mo><mtext>Take</mtext><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: An LCR series circuit of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>62</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nF</mi></math> and resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, is connected to an A.C. source of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>. For maximum value of amplitude of current in circuit, the value of inductance is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mH</mi></math>.<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open=""><mrow><mo>(</mo><mtext>Take</mtext><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Given here, f=2 kHz=2000 Hz, C=62.5 nF=62.5×10-9 F and R=50 Ω

Resonance frequency (amplitude of the current will be maximum in this case) is given by f=12πLC, here, L is the inductance.

Putting the values, 

2000 Hz=12πL×62.5×10-9

L=14π2×20002×62.5×10-9=0.1 H=100 mH

subject: Physics
q: An inductor stores 16 J of magnetic field energy and dissipates 32 W of thermal energy due to its resistance when an a.c. current of 2 A (rms) and frequency 50 Hz flows through it. The ratio of inductive reactance to its resistance is $\_\_\_\_$. $(\pi=3.14)$
question: An inductor stores 16 J of magnetic field energy and dissipates 32 W of thermal energy due to its resistance when an a.c. current of 2 A (rms) and frequency 50 Hz flows through it. The ratio of inductive reactance to its resistance is $\_\_\_\_$. $(\pi=3.14)$
solution:
Given: Magnetic field energy $= 16$ J, Thermal power dissipated $= 32$ W, $I_{rms} = 2$ A, $f = 50$ Hz.
From energy stored in inductor:
$\frac{1}{2}L I_{rms}^2 = 16$
$\frac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot 4 = 16$
$L = 8 \text{ H}$
From thermal power dissipated:
$I_{rms}^2 R = 32$
$4R = 32$
$R = 8 \; \Omega$
Inductive reactance:
$X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 8 = 2512 \; \Omega$
$\frac{X_L}{R} = \frac{2512}{8} = 314$

subject: Physics
q: A series <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> circuit containing an inductor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mH</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> a capacitor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>120</mn><mtext> μ</mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and a resistor <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is driven by an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The energy dissipated in the circuit in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
question: A series <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> circuit containing an inductor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mH</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> a capacitor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>120</mn><mtext> μ</mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and a resistor <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is driven by an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The energy dissipated in the circuit in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
solution:
(Power factor), Pf=cos⁡ϕ=RZ

Where R, Z are the resistance and impedance respectively.
(Energy dissipated) E=Vrms2Z×cosϕ×t
⇒E=Vrms2Z×RZ×t

⇒E=Vrms2Z2×R×t

Vrms, t are the RMS voltage and time respectively.

We know that the Impedance of a LCR circuit is given by the formula,
     Z2=R2+ωL-1ωC2⇒Z2=602+2π×50×20×10-3-12π×50×120×10-62

Where ω, L, C are the angular frequency, inductance and capacitance in the circuit respectively.
⇒Z2=602+2π-100012π2

⇒Z2=4009.7

∴E=2424009.7×60×60 J 

= 517.14 J

=5.17×102 J

q: In an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></math> series circuit, an inductor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> and a resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are connected to an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AC</mi></math> source of angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of capacitance for which the current leads the voltage by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: In an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></math> series circuit, an inductor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> and a resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are connected to an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AC</mi></math> source of angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of capacitance for which the current leads the voltage by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
tanϕ=xC-xLR
tan45=xC-xLR
xC-xL=R
1ωC-ωL=R
1ωC-300×0.03=1
1ωC=10
C=110ω=110×300
C=13×10-3
X=3

subject: Physics
q: An electric bulb rated as $100 \mathrm{~W}-220 \mathrm{~V}$ is connected to an ac source of rms voltage 220 V. The peak value of current through the bulb is :
question: An electric bulb rated as $100 \mathrm{~W}-220 \mathrm{~V}$ is connected to an ac source of rms voltage 220 V. The peak value of current through the bulb is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}} \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{rms}} \\ & \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{100}{220} \\ & \mathrm{i}_0=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{rms}}=0.64 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math> of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> is applied to a parallel plate capacitor. The separation between the plates is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the area is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The amplitude of the oscillating displacement current for the applied <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> voltage is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mtext>Take </mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math> of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> is applied to a parallel plate capacitor. The separation between the plates is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the area is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The amplitude of the oscillating displacement current for the applied <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> voltage is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mtext>Take </mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
From the given information,

C=∈0 A d=ϵ0×12×10-3 F
∴ XC=1ωC=2×10-32×50π×∈0=2×10-325×4π∈0 Ω
∴ XC=2×10-325×9×109=1825×106 Ω
∴ i0=V0XC=20×2518×10-6 A=27.47 μA.

The value of amplitude of displacement current will be same as value of amplitude of conventional current.
Hence option 3.

[Take π as 227
question:
A 0.07 H inductor and a 12 Ω resistor are connected in series to a 220 V, 50 Hz AC source. The approximate current in the circuit and the phase angle between current and source voltage are respectively.

[Take π as 227
solution:
ϕ=tan-1XLR

XL=ωL 

XL=2×227×50×0.07=22 Ω

ϕ=tan-12212  R=12 Ω

ϕ=tan-1116
Z=XL2+R2=25.059
I=VZ=22025.059=8.77 A

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : When the frequency of an AC source in a series LCR circuit increases, the current in the circuit first increases, attains a maximum value and then decreases.

Statement II : In a series LCR circuit, the value of power factor at resonance is one.

In the light of given statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : When the frequency of an AC source in a series LCR circuit increases, the current in the circuit first increases, attains a maximum value and then decreases.

Statement II : In a series LCR circuit, the value of power factor at resonance is one.

In the light of given statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is correct but Statement II is false
solution:
When an a.c. voltage is applied in a series LCR circuit, the current is increased first, attains a maximum and then decreases.

The formula to calculate the power factor is given by

cosϕ=RR2+XL2-XC2   ...1

At resonance, XL=XC, Hence,

cosϕresonance=1

Thus, both the statements are true.
